questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Faraday's experiment proved the existence of electrons
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Faraday's experiments, particularly his work on electromagnetism and electrochemistry, did not directly prove the existence of electrons. His experiments focused on the relationship between electricity and magnetism, as well as the phenomenon of electrolysis. The discovery of electrons came later through the work of J.J. Thomson and his cathode ray experiments.

options.text: Crookes tube experiment shows the presence of electrons and protons in the atoms
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The Crookes tube experiment, conducted by Sir William Crookes, aimed to study the properties of cathode rays. Although the experiment provided evidence for the existence of negatively charged particles (later identified as electrons), it did not demonstrate the presence of protons in atoms. The discovery of protons as constituents of the atomic nucleus came later through the experiments conducted by Ernest Rutherford.

options.text: Radioactivity confirms the presence of electrons and protons
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Radioactivity, the spontaneous emission of particles or radiation from certain elements, does not directly confirm the presence of electrons and protons. Radioactivity is primarily associated with the decay or transmutation of atomic nuclei, involving processes such as alpha decay, beta decay, or gamma radiation. These phenomena are related to the instability of atomic nuclei and the release of specific particles or energy, but they do not directly provide evidence for the presence of electrons and protons.

options.text: Chadwick experiment shows the presence of neutrons
options.explanation: Chadwick carried out his own experiments and confirmed that he had finally found the neutron. Chadwick bombarded boron with alpha particles which emitted neutral rays, like beryllium, and placed a hydrogen target in the path of the rays.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct. Chadwick bombarded a sample of beryllium with alpha particles and observed a new form of radiation that was not affected by electric fields. He concluded that this radiation consisted of neutral particles, which he named neutrons. Neutrons have no electric charge but have a mass similar to protons.

questions.explanation: The maximum number of electrons in a subshell is given by the formula 2n², where "n" represents the principal quantum number of the subshell. In the context of the electron configuration of atoms, subshells are defined by the azimuthal quantum number (also known as the orbital angular momentum quantum number), denoted by the letters s, p, d, f, and so on. Each subshell can accommodate a specific maximum number of electrons.For example:- The s subshell (with n = 1) can hold a maximum of 2 electrons (2 x 1² = 2).- The p subshell (with n = 2) can hold a maximum of 6 electrons (2 x 2² = 8).- The d subshell (with n = 3) can hold a maximum of 10 electrons (2 x 3² = 18).- The f subshell (with n = 4) can hold a maximum of 14 electrons (2 x 4² = 32).This formula is based on the Pauli exclusion principle and the rules of quantum mechanics, which dictate the allowed number of electrons in each subshell and the filling order of orbitals within the subshells.L= azimuthal quantum numberL=n-1, where n is the principal quantum number Some values of l are as follows:s=0, p=1, d=2, f=3The numbers of orbitals in a subshell are equal to 2l+1Each orbital can hold a maximum of 2 electrons, so the maximum number of electrons in a subshell can be calculated using 2(2l+1)Therefore, the correct answer is B.(Source: PCTB page no. 143)

questions.text: The particle with highest e/m ratio
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The particle with the highest e/m ratio is the electron. The e/m ratio is the charge-to-mass ratio of a particle. The electron has a charge of 1.602 × 10^-19 coulombs and a mass of 9.109 × 10^-31 kilograms. This gives the electron an e/m ratio of 1.7588 × 10^8 coulombs per kilogram. The high e/m ratio of the electron makes it a very important particle in physics. It is used in many different experiments, including the study of the structure of atoms and molecules. The electron is also responsible for many of the properties of matter, such as its electrical conductivity and its ability to absorb and emit light.
Proton: The proton has a charge of 1.602 × 10^-19 coulombs and a mass of 1.6726 × 10^-27 kilograms. This gives the proton an e/m ratio of 9.5784 × 10^7 coulombs per kilogram.
Neutron: The neutron has a charge of 0 coulombs and a mass of 1.6749 × 10^-27 kilograms. This gives the neutron an e/m ratio of 0 coulombs per kilogram.

Meson: The meson is a group of particles that have a mass that is intermediate between the mass of an electron and the mass of a proton. The e/m ratio of a meson varies depending on the type of meson.

questions.text: Orbitals having the same energy are called:

options.explanation: Hybrid orbitals are a concept in chemistry where atomic orbitals mix to create new orbitals suitable for bonding. They are not specifically orbitals with the same energy.
Degenerate orbitals are orbitals within the same subshell that have equal energy levels. These orbitals remain degenerate unless influenced by external factors like electric or magnetic fields.
Valence orbitals are the outermost orbitals that can participate in chemical bonding by accepting electrons. They are not defined by having the same energy.
Sub-orbitals refer to the specific orbitals within the s, p, d, and f subshells, characterized by unique quantum numbers. They do not necessarily have the same energy.

Other options like hybrid orbitals, valence orbitals, and sub-orbitals describe different concepts:Hybrid orbitals: Result from the mixing of atomic orbitals for bonding purposes, not defined by energy equality.Valence orbitals: Located in the outer shell and involved in bonding, but not characterized by having the same energy.Sub-orbitals: Specific orbitals within subshells like s, p, d, and f, each with distinct quantum numbers, not necessarily sharing the same energy.

questions.text: The number of protons in the nucleus is called the _ number.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The atomic number is the number of protons present in the nucleus of an atom. It is a fundamental property of an element and uniquely identifies each element on the periodic table. For example, hydrogen has an atomic number of 1, meaning it has one proton in its nucleus; helium has an atomic number of 2, meaning it has two protons, and so on.

Charge refers to the electrical property of particles, which can be positive, negative, or neutral. While protons carry a positive charge (+1), electrons carry a negative charge (-1), and neutrons are neutral (no charge). The number of protons and electrons in an atom are equal, resulting in a net charge of zero for a neutral atom.

The answer choice "Atomic number or charge" appears to be a combination of both options (a) and (b). The atomic number specifically refers to the number of protons in the nucleus, while charge refers to the overall electrical charge of the atom. While both concepts are related, they are not interchangeable.

questions.text: Hund's rule is applicable to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Each orbital in a sublevel is separately occupied before any orbital is doubly occupied. All of the electrons in separately occupied orbitals have an equivalent spin (to maximize total spin). If we individually write the electronic configurations and distribute the electrons in sub-orbitals of 2p (2px, 2py, 2pz), the electrons would be distributed according to Hund's rule.

Oxygen has an atomic number of 8, and its electron configuration is 1s² 2s² 2p⁴. According to Hund's Rule, electrons will occupy separate orbitals within a subshell before pairing up. In the case of oxygen, the three 2p orbitals will each have one electron before any of them have two.

c. Nitrogen: Nitrogen has an atomic number of 7, and its electron configuration is 1s² 2s² 2p³. Similarly, the three 2p orbitals are filled with one electron each before any of them have two, following Hund's Rule.

d. Neon: Neon has an atomic number of 10, and its electron configuration is 1s² 2s² 2p⁶. In this case, all the available orbitals in the 2p subshell are fully filled, and there are no unpaired electrons. Hund's Rule is still applicable here, as it was followed in the process of filling the orbitals.

e. Carbon: Carbon has an atomic number of 6, and its electron configuration is 1s² 2s² 2p². Hund's Rule is applicable here as well because the two electrons in the 2p orbitals are arranged singly before pairing up.

questions.explanation: According to Hund's rule: Each orbital in a sublevel is separately occupied before any orbital is doubly occupied. All of the electrons in separately occupied orbitals have an equivalent spin (to maximize total spin). If we individually write the electronic configurations and distribute the electrons in sub-orbitals of 2p (2px, 2py, 2pz), the electrons would be distributed according to Hund's rule.

questions.text: Positive rays are produced when _ strike the molecules of a gas in a discharge tube.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Low-speed neutrons: Neutrons are neutral particles, so they do not have any charge. They also have a very small mass, so they do not have enough energy to knock out electrons from gas molecules. Therefore, low-speed neutrons cannot produce positive rays.

High-speed neutrons: High-speed neutrons are also neutral particles, but they have a lot of energy. However, they are not affected by electric or magnetic fields, so they cannot be used to produce positive rays.
Low-speed electrons: Low-speed electrons do not have enough energy to knock out electrons from gas molecules. Therefore, low-speed electrons cannot produce positive rays.

Positive rays are produced when high-speed electrons strike the molecules of a gas in a discharge tube. When a high-speed electron strikes a molecule of gas, it can knock out one or more electrons from the molecule. The molecule that has lost electrons becomes a positively charged ion. These positively charged ions are called positive rays. The positive rays are attracted to the negative electrode in the discharge tube, and they can be deflected by a magnetic field. This deflection can be used to measure the charge-to-mass ratio of the positive rays.

questions.explanation: In the Discharge Tube Experiment, high-speed electrons transition from the metal of the cathode and go towards the anode. It may collide with gas molecules present in the tube. Out of many possibilities, one being the gas molecule may lose an electron (pushed out) upon collision, forming a stream of positively charged particles, also known as anode rays. Hence, the particles responsible for the production of positive rays are "high-speed electrons".

questions.text: Which character of p-orbital determines the geometry of molecules?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This term typically refers to a flat, two-dimensional arrangement. In the context of molecular geometry, certain molecules exhibit planar geometry. For example, molecules with a central atom surrounded by three identical bonding pairs (trigonal planar) or molecules with four identical atoms or groups in a plane (square planar) have planar geometries. The p-orbitals may contribute to the overall geometry in such cases.

Axial generally refers to positions along a central axis. In molecular geometry, this term may be associated with the arrangement of atoms or groups in a specific direction. For instance, in molecules with a trigonal bipyramidal geometry, there are axial and equatorial positions. The axial positions are aligned along the axis perpendicular to the plane formed by the equatorial positions. P-orbitals can contribute to the axial positions in certain cases.

This term suggests that there is no specific direction associated with the character of the p-orbital. P-orbitals are generally directional in nature, but when they undergo hybridization, the resulting hybrid orbitals (such as sp3 hybrid orbitals) may have a more spherical distribution, reducing the directional nature of the original p-orbitals.

A p-orbital has two lobes like a dumbell and it has a strong directional character. There are three types of p-orbitals depending on the direction of lobes/their orientation in space along either x or y or z axis. When a bond is formed between two atoms by the overlap of two p orbitals or a p-orbital and an s orbital, the direction of the bond depends on the direction of p-orbital. A covalent bond is rigid and directional. The geometry of a molecule depends on the arrangement of the bonds and hence on the orientation of the orbitals involved in bonding.

questions.text: Quantum number which describes the orientation of orbitals in three dimensional space is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The Spin Quantum Number (ms) describes the angular momentum of an electron. An electron spins around an axis and has both angular momentum and orbital angular momentum. Because angular momentum is a vector, the Spin Quantum Number (s) has both a magnitude (1/2) and direction (+ or -).

The azimuthal (or orbital angular momentum) quantum number describes the shape of a given orbital. It is denoted by the symbol 'l'.A value of the azimuthal quantum number can indicate either an s, p, d, or f subshell which vary in shape.

Magnetic Quantum Number Specifies the orientation in space of an orbital of a given energy (n) and shape (l). This number divides the subshell into individual orbitals which hold the electrons; there are 2l+1 orbitals in each subshell.

The principal quantum number, n, describes the energy of an electron and the most probable distance of the electron from the nucleus. In other words, it refers to the size of the orbital and the energy level an electron is placed in.

questions.explanation: Magnetic Quantum Number (m) : Gives the orientation of the orbital in space; in other words, the value of m describes whether an orbital lies along the x-, y-, or z-axis on a three-dimensional graph, with the nucleus of the atom at the origin. m can take on any value from −ltol. Hence option C is correct.
